summary: Possible typosquat of 'ulid'/'ulidx' with mismatched install instructions and oversized dependency closure
details: Package name `ulid-xyz` closely resembles the popular `ulid` and `ulidx` npm packages. The README appears copied from upstream and instructs users to `npm install index-ulid` and `import { ulid } from "index-ulid"` — a third, different name that does not match the published package name. The homepage points at the legitimate upstream project (github.com/ulid/javascript). The shipped code in `dist/node/index.cjs` and ESM/browser siblings is a faithful re-export of the standard ULID API (`ulid`, `monotonicFactory`, `decodeTime`, `ulidToUUID`, etc.) and uses only `node:crypto` — no network I/O, no `eval`/`Function`, no `child_process`, no filesystem access to installer secrets, and no `preinstall`/`install`/`postinstall`/`prepare` lifecycle scripts. Separately, `package.json` declares heavyweight runtime `dependencies` (`pino`, `ws`, `postinstall`, `esbuild`, `ts-node`, `zod`, `@types/node`, `@types/ws`, `tsup`, `typescript`) that the shipped dist code does not import — a ULID generator only needs `node:crypto`. None of these deps are known-malicious, but the combination of a confusable name, a README that points at yet a third install name, replicated public API, and an oversized dependency closure for a ~200-LOC utility is the typosquat shape. The current 3.2.1 payload itself does not exfiltrate, execute remote code, or steal credentials; the concern is name confusion and future-version risk under a maintainer outside the legitimate project.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
